quickFacts: Development means improving people's quality of life across economic, social and environmental dimensions.
Economic development includes jobs, income, trade, industry and infrastructure.
Social development includes health, education, safety, housing, equality and freedom.
Environmental development includes clean air/water, biodiversity and sustainable land and energy use.
HDI combines life expectancy, education and GNI per capita.
Gini measures inequality: 0 = equal, 1 = totally unequal.
Development is a journey, not a label: countries can improve or regress over time.
LIC incomes can be low because countries rely on low-value farming or raw materials, weak infrastructure, conflict or poor governance.
Food security means physical and economic access to sufficient, safe, nutritious food at all times.
Malthus argued population grows faster than food supply, but the Green Revolution showed technology can increase yields.
The modern food problem is often access, distribution, waste, conflict and poverty, not only production.
In the Sahel, low rainfall and overgrazing remove vegetation, expose soil and cause desertification.
In Yemen, conflict damages farms, roads, ports and markets, reducing food supply and raising prices.
Water is essential because it improves health, school attendance, farming, jobs and income.
Physical water scarcity means not enough water exists; economic water scarcity means water exists but people cannot access it.
Ethiopia wants GERD for electricity and development, while Egypt worries about reduced Nile water.
Trade can create jobs and export income, but can also increase inequality if workers are underpaid.
Raw-material exports usually earn less than manufactured or high-value exports.
Rana Plaza shows the human cost of cheap fast fashion and weak worker protection.
Health and development are linked because sick people miss school and work, reducing income.
Malaria slows development because it causes illness, school absence, work absence and treatment costs.
Strong 5–7 mark answers use PEEL, specific evidence and a final judgement/development link.
Source answers need exact evidence: number, place, trend or pattern.
The golden chain is cause → effect → development impact.
No single indicator is perfect; geographers combine GDP, GNP, life expectancy, literacy, HDI, GII, Gini and wellbeing data.
Global North and Global South are useful patterns but have exceptions such as Australia and oil-rich Gulf states.
Physical factors such as drought, landlocked location, hazards and poor soils interact with human factors such as colonialism, trade, governance and conflict.
Development pathways often move from agriculture to industry to services and knowledge, but shocks can slow or reverse progress.
Fast fashion has social costs for workers and environmental costs from water use, chemical dyes, microplastics and landfill waste.

stats.stat: Top 10% own 59% of wealth; bottom 50% own 2%; top 1% typical net worth about $6.2m vs median NZer $92k.
HDI ranges 0–1. Very high: 0.80–1.00. Low: below 0.55. NZ about 0.930; Ethiopia about 0.492; South Sudan about 0.385.
Population heading to ~11b by 2100. Cereal production rose 3.5× since 1961, population 2.6×. Food waste about 1/3.
Semi-arid belt; rainfall about 200–600mm/year. Stone bunds can increase yields by up to 50%.
War since 2015; real GDP per capita down nearly 60%; Hudaydah port about 25% capacity; 600k+ under-5s acutely malnourished; aid funding about 9%.
Sudan conflict from Apr 2023; 7m+ displaced; 18m food insecure in Sudan, 7m in South Sudan; Sudan food prices up 60%+.
Norway 100%, NZ 95.6%, Brazil 73.1%, India 62%, Nigeria 23.7%, Ethiopia 12%. Egypt relies on Nile for about 95% of freshwater.
24 Apr 2013, Savar, Bangladesh; 1,134 killed, 2,500+ injured; workers earned under US$100/month; Accord covered 1,600+ factories.
~about 250 million+ malaria cases globally each year; about 94% of cases/deaths occur in Sub-Saharan Africa; under-5s about 75–80% of deaths in the African Region; Nigeria loses about US$1b/year.
China lifted 800m+ from poverty; Vietnam poverty fell from 70% in the 1990s to under 6%; DRC cobalt shows resource curse risk.

cases.name: Sahel food supply
cases.location: Semi-arid belt south of the Sahara, across Mali, Niger, Chad, Sudan and nearby countries.
cases.problem: Food supply is unreliable because rainfall is low and variable, soils are fragile, and many people depend on subsistence farming and herding.
responses: Drought-tolerant crops such as millet and sorghum
stats: Stone bunds can raise yields up to 50%
cases.gold: Low rainfall reduces vegetation; overgrazing removes the remaining plants; bare soil is eroded, causing desertification and reducing food supply.

cases.name: Yemen food crisis
cases.problem: A severe food crisis mainly caused by conflict damaging farms, roads, ports, markets and health systems.
stats: real GDP per capita down nearly 60% since 2015
cases.gold: Conflict attacks all three food-security pillars: availability, access and utilisation.
cases.probReason: Top predicted food case: conflict, causes, impacts and responses.

cases.name: Sudan and South Sudan food crisis
cases.location: Northeast Africa; Nile system, desert north and seasonal rainfall south.
cases.problem: Conflict, displacement, droughts, floods and inflation have created major food insecurity.
stats: 18m food insecure in Sudan; 7m in South Sudan
cases.gold: Both climate and conflict matter, but conflict stops people farming, trading and receiving aid.

cases.name: Nile / GERD
cases.location: GERD is on the Blue Nile in Ethiopia; Egypt is downstream.
cases.problem: Ethiopia wants hydroelectric power and development, while Egypt fears reduced water for farming, drinking and industry.
impacts: Risk to farming in dry years
stats: Egypt relies on Nile for about 95% of freshwater
cases.gold: Water can cause conflict, but cooperation is possible through shared data and agreed releases.

cases.name: Aral Sea
cases.location: Central Asia, between Kazakhstan and Uzbekistan.
cases.problem: Rivers feeding the sea were diverted for cotton irrigation, causing the sea to shrink.
responses: Kok-Aral Dam restoration in the north
stats: Sea shrank to about 10% of original volume
Kok-Aral Dam helped North Aral partially recover
cases.gold: The Aral Sea shows how poor water management can damage health, jobs and ecosystems.

cases.name: Rana Plaza / fast fashion
cases.problem: Fast fashion creates jobs and export income but can rely on low wages and unsafe factories.
cases.gold: Trade supports development only when work is safe, fairly paid and protected.

cases.name: Raw-material exports / DRC
cases.problem: Countries can remain poor if they export raw materials cheaply instead of higher-value goods.
stats: DRC has valuable cobalt for phones and EV batteries
cases.gold: Raw materials usually earn less than manufactured goods, so LICs need value-added exports.

cases.name: Malaria in Sub-Saharan Africa
cases.location: Warm, wet parts of Sub-Saharan Africa.
cases.problem: Malaria causes illness and death and reduces school and work attendance.
stats: about 250 million+ malaria cases globally each year
about 94% of cases/deaths occur in Sub-Saharan Africa
children under 5 are about 80% of deaths
cases.gold: Malaria is both a cause and consequence of underdevelopment.

cases.name: NZ wealth inequality
cases.problem: A country can be high income but still have serious internal inequality.
responses: Better access to education and health
cases.gold: HDI can hide internal inequalities, so geographers also use Gini and wealth data.

cases.name: Brazil favelas / internal inequality
cases.location: Urban Brazil, especially large cities where wealthy areas and informal settlements can exist close together.
cases.problem: Favelas show that inequality can exist within countries, not only between rich and poor countries.
stats: Useful comparison with New Zealand internal inequality
Shows national averages such as HDI can hide local inequality
cases.gold: Brazil shows that development gaps can exist inside one country, so national averages can hide inequality.

cases.name: New Zealand vs Ethiopia development pathway
cases.location: New Zealand as a high-income country; Ethiopia as a lower-HDI country in transition.
cases.problem: The two countries show different development pathways, indicators and demographic stages.
causes: NZ: services, education, healthcare and infrastructure
Ethiopia: small-scale agriculture, rapid population growth and climate vulnerability
Different trade patterns and public investment
impacts: NZ: ageing population and high living standards
Ethiopia: pressure on land, jobs and services
Different health, education and income outcomes
responses: Improve roads, clinics and clean water
stats: NZ HDI about 0.930; Ethiopia HDI about 0.492
cases.gold: Development is a journey, not a label: countries can improve over time but still face different barriers.

cases.name: China and Vietnam trade winners
cases.problem: Export-led growth can reduce poverty, but benefits may be uneven.
impacts: Vietnam poverty fell from about 70% to under 6%
Pollution, low wages and inequality remain
stats: China 800m+ lifted from poverty; Vietnam poverty 70% to under 6%
cases.gold: Trade can support development when countries use export income to build skills, infrastructure and higher-value industries.

cases.name: LIC vs HIC health patterns
cases.location: Comparison across countries at different development levels.
cases.problem: LICs often face more communicable diseases, while HICs often face more non-communicable diseases.
causes: LICs may have weaker sanitation, clean water access and healthcare
LICs may have lower vaccination and treatment access
HICs have longer life expectancy and ageing populations
Lifestyle factors can increase NCDs in HICs
impacts: Communicable diseases can cause school and work absence in LICs
Poor health can lower income and productivity
HICs may need long-term care for ageing populations
Health inequality creates different development priorities
responses: Prevention and treatment of both communicable and non-communicable diseases
cases.gold: Health patterns reflect development: LICs often face infectious diseases, while HICs often face ageing and lifestyle-related diseases.
cases.probReason: Likely second health case and pattern question.
